text:
Magnesium Joint Sachet
The magnesium filings were saved.  I placed them in a piece of paper towel.  I laid a bare stranded copper wire in the filings.  Then rolled the group like a joint.  I then bent the metal joint in half and sealed it shut with a second piece of copper.  I completed the magnesium sachet by twisting both wires together.  I then tested the magnesium sachet with carbon in a tap water bath.  The amps came in at 1.3 milliamperes.  Again, the shaving have points and powered metal has alot of surface area by mass.  This was a very small amount.  This is the technique of modern battery manufacturing.  It works well.

author: @meex88hiphop
text:
AI response:
"In the case of alternating aluminum and brass tubes, with aluminum downstream and brass upstream in moving water, there may still be potential for the Seebeck effect to generate electricity. This setup can create a temperature gradient between the two metals due to the difference in thermal conductivity between aluminum and brass. The aluminum being less thermally conductive will retain heat longer, and thus, it will be warmer downstream than upstream. The brass, being more thermally conductive, will quickly transfer the heat to the water, resulting in a lower temperature downstream than upstream. This temperature difference can create an electrical potential difference between the two metals, which can generate an electric current.

However, the effectiveness of this setup will depend on several factors, such as the temperature difference between the two metals, the flow rate of the water, and the length and thickness of the tubes. Additionally, the setup will need to be properly insulated to prevent electrical losses due to contact with the surrounding water."

"In still water, the use of alternating aluminum and brass tubes may not generate a significant amount of electricity through the Seebeck effect. The Seebeck effect relies on the presence of a temperature gradient between two different metals, which is generated by a flow of heat. In still water, there is no flow of heat, so there is no temperature gradient generated between the aluminum and brass tubes."

Q: brass or copper?

A: "Both brass and copper can be used to generate electricity through the Seebeck effect in alternating metal tube setups.

Brass is an alloy made of copper and zinc, and it has good thermal conductivity and is relatively corrosion-resistant, which can make it a good choice for use in water. However, brass has lower electrical conductivity than copper, which can affect the efficiency of the Seebeck effect in generating electricity.

Copper, on the other hand, has higher electrical conductivity than brass, which can make it a more efficient material for generating electricity through the Seebeck effect. However, copper is more susceptible to corrosion and may require additional protection in wet or corrosive environments."

Q: "least heat conductive metal with good electrical conductivity"

A: "One metal that fits the criteria of having a low thermal conductivity while maintaining a good electrical conductivity is nichrome. Nichrome is an alloy composed of nickel, chromium, and iron, and it is commonly used as a heating element due to its high resistance to electrical current and low thermal conductivity. It is often used in appliances such as toasters and hair dryers because it can get very hot without transferring too much heat to the surrounding materials. Additionally, nichrome has a relatively high electrical conductivity compared to other metals with low thermal conductivity, making it a good choice for applications where both electrical and thermal properties are important."

Q: "best heat conductive metal with good electrical conductivity"

A: "Copper is one of the best heat conductive metals with good electrical conductivity. It has a thermal conductivity of about 400 W/mK (watts per meter Kelvin) and an electrical conductivity of about 58.0 × 10^6 S/m (Siemens per meter) at room temperature. Copper is widely used in electrical applications, such as wiring and circuit boards, as well as in heat exchangers and other heat transfer applications, such as cooking pots and pans, due to its excellent thermal conductivity. Silver is an even better conductor of heat than copper, but it is less commonly used due to its high cost."

This is nothing more than a standard battery using 2 different metals. You can do the same with a zinc and copper rod stuck into a lemon.
So long as there are some electrolytes in the water to allow conduction, you will get an EMF (which depends on the electronegativity of the two metals used) and the current will be pretty miniscule - not enough to run most devices.
But it makes absolutely no difference which rod is upstream.

author: @jangloersen2691
text:
Just saw this video. As I just completed building a Brown's gas electrolyzer of George Wiseman's super efficient design I couldn't help wondering upon his claim of Brown's gas containing Electrically Expanded Water (ExW), his claim, which according to him is a liquid water that has turned into plasma (gas with electrons) BETWEEN the electrolyzer plates. 

He claims ExW does not condense back to water upon cooling, thus BG contains cold plasma. 

If what is claimed in THIA patent which you animate, that current is generated from upstream to downstream electron flow is correct, it shouldn't matter if these electrons are transported by cold plasma or liquid, should it?

Sadly I don't have not can I afford to buy a sensitive ammeter, but if you happen to have BG generator laying about, this should be a way to at least give an indication if George Wiseman's might be correct in his claim.

Bubbling the gas through distilled water (twice) to remove traces of electrolyte (NaOH in my case) before having it flow past the Aluminium/ Brass electrodes would be prudent I suppose to remove suspicion that traces of the electrolyte would be the source of conductivity. 

Related to this I guess one should then compare amp output from twice scrubbed BG (cold electron plasma) vs distilled water from the second or even a third that has been bubbled for at least 10 minutes per liter of distilled water with a 1l/min BG output from generator vs tap water.

author: @Wavicle
text:
You've misunderstood the patent. The patent is for a generator, not a battery. It doesn't work if the water is still or the water temperature is the same for both electrodes; and it doesn't work if the water doesn't contain enough impurities to conduct electricity. It depends on the availability of free electrons between the electrodes; you only get electric current flow if the number of free electrons at each electrode is different. It isn't perpetual, because it takes energy to increase the number of free electrons at one electrode. Agitating one electrode has the same effect as putting that electrode in faster moving water - which would increase the number free electrons that electrode is exposed to.

A much easier way to get the same effect is to use a thermopile. It works on a similar principle but depends instead on the electron mobility within different metals. They can be had for $5.

author: @gulutaalan8845
text: C'mon! You know why water is said to have Ph 7? Cause even in ultra-pure distilled water, there are 10^-7 moles/liter of ions continuously forming by autoprotolysis and neutralizing each other. The water you use surely has more (not speaking about the river one, that's an electrolyte).  As long as you have TWO different metals, a tiny fraction of these ions will react in an asymmetric way to the two metals having different electrochemical potential and, at small distances, this tiny corrosion (with microamps you won't notice it) forms nothing but a battery which might last decades. Just think a bit: in a normal battery you start seeing corrosion after days at tens of mA, the metal amount is less than that you used and a lot more active. Aluminum is always covered by oxide and this also slows down the process. Brass is also quite corrosion resistant. This battery will show minimal corrosion (if you keep the circuit in short) after many years. The "fluid movement" is not so relevant, what you change there is the distance and therefore, the resistance. The temperature matters. Don't use wrong titles, this is not a perpetual battery, is just an useless battery.

author: @taylorwestmore4664
text: Charge seperation occurs in water-air interface due to photoelectric effect and the liquid crystal state of water having a dipole moment oriented away from the interface. High surface area Super hydrophillic materials can cause the liquid crystal state of water to exclude charges, the "charge exclusion zone liquid crystalline water interface" or "EZ water" for short in the literature. This charge seperation can be tapped for power which allows ions in the bulk to move back into the exclusion barrier, only to be forced out again by solar radiation or convective mixing. If you make layers of transparent and conductive super hydrophillic plastic with water between them, indeed you will have a water based volumetric solar photovoltaic cell with a high efficiency and a slight pseudo-capacitive effect based on the distance between the water cells. Please do more experiments with EZ water!

text: Looks like an alternative to the discovery of Stan Meyers with his "electric amplifier" device.  No one has reproduced it successfully to date that I am aware of.  It would be good to try this out in a loop of plastic pipe with a small impeller pump.  You could place several plate pairs through the center at slight angles to create a tiny bit of turbulence if it helps.  This power generation is most likely pulling charges from the air above the water after depleting the charges carried by the water directly.  If this is true, you could detect it by testing with a negative ion generator near the water and/or testing the air temperature at the surface with a sensitive probe.  The temp should slightly decrease as energy is pulled away.  If it is pulling from the air, put your plates under a water vortex of a Viktor Schauberger type funnel.  That should amplify, by an order of magnitude, your resulting power by increasing the surface area and velocity greatly.

author: @Lotharyx
text: This is simple galvanic action.  You’ve made an ordinary battery; nothing more, nothing less. Two dissimilar conductors placed in an electrolyte will develop a potential difference between them as electrons migrate toward the conductor with the more positive electrode potential.  This isn’t free or perpetual; the flow of electrons is driven by electrochemical reactions happening at one or both conductors.  Heating adds energy to the system, speeding up the reaction and increasing the rate of electron transfer.  Agitation and electrolyte flow help to replenish the unreacted species, also increasing current flow.  Left alone long enough in a closed system, all available reducible species will be consumed and current flow will cease.

author: @CDinkle
text: I'm sure you thought of this, but you can increase the amount of surface electrons by increasing the surface area. In this case, using a spring (or coiled wire) with a coil diameter that is equal to the tube diameter, and a gauge diameter that is the same as the tube thickness (OD-ID)/2, would increase the surface area. I ran a quick algebraic comparison of surface areas in MATLAB and it's not very much (~5% increase), but you could probably vary the gauge diameter and coil diameter to get better results.

replies.author: @biffaozzie2373
replies.text: Hi Robert.  My concern here starts with the title-  "powered by water ",  which sounds similar to those people that think they can power a car with water.  Or, the videos where people make a battery out of a lemon, and seem to think that the power is coming from the lemon somehow.  Your device is a battery and is powered by the electrochemical potential between the aluminium and the copper/zinc.  When you waggle it you are stirring the surface layer and making it slightly more efficient.  That's all, no "power" is coming from the waggling. When you dip it in the river, turn it round so either the brass or the aluminium is upstream- there will be no difference (please make a video, I'd be interested to see the result). To be fair, I haven't read the patent, but it's my experience that you can patent almost anything (eg US6368227B1 ).  Disclaimer- I have undergrad level physics and a  PhD in biophysics.  I quite enjoy your videos, but sometimes I think you are on thin ice.  Regards...

replies.author: @DanielSMatthews
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering Yeah the bit about it not being magnetohydrodynamics is a specific claim, but as to what it really is I'm still not sure, at one point it mentions a conducting liquid, but pure water is not a conductor. So is an electrolyte is really required? Here is an interesting experiment then. A closed box with a turbine attached to a magnetic clutch such that an additional external turbine in a stream of any liquid is able to rotate the internal turbine which in turn causes an electrolyte to flow around a circular path and between inert electrodes of different conductivity, N.B. gold conductivity vs platinum. If that works you can exclude certain possible explanations for how the physics of it may work. You could build this test rig with a 3D printer, some neodymium magnets (make sure they are sealed away from the liquids!) and the appropriate electrodes, plus salt and water. I have an old PCI card here with gold on the connector, which I could hack off) but that is plated onto copper, and I don't even have platinum as wire, or in any form. The inert electrodes are important to eliminate electrochemical causes for the current flow, I guess the electrolyte needs to be one that is the least likely to attack the electrodes even though they are inert, so a base rather than an acid or a salt is even better?

author: @tdtrecordsmusic
text: I noticed the same thing years back working with scariac's.  I just used the mag stir bar. Plus, I thought I had same material for both electrodes.  Stainless probably. My research papers in that stint were melted by a flood = gone.  However, I do remember that my hypothesis is that the effect is due to the triboelectric effect/static.  Was planning on using pipes to explore further.  Consider a 2 legged experiment where one set was using PVC couplings(series) and one set using parallel paths with T-couplings. Conductive pipes with liquid running inside, separated by non-conductive couplings.   That notion eludes to yet another hypothesis: There is likely a 'micro-organization' in the fluid which is short circuiting  b/c we cannot make wires out of soup. Hence potential will not build b/c there is no separation to create the potential 'difference'

replies.author: @G-ra-ha-m
replies.text:
Redrooster That all depends upon _where_ the energy is coming from. Hooking a cable up to the ionosphere (perhaps building a plasma cable with a giant Tesla coil in WardenClyffe) would give you 'free' energy, but that doesn't mean it comes from nowhere. 
Water is funny stuff and has a variety of properties that vary with contamination, have a look at this video:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YxerfsaEPYs
and see what you think. Sometimes boring stuff like water is ignored because we 'know all about it', until (for example) some bright spark has a closer look and discovered it's made of liquid crystals and surface tension can be used by more than pond skater insects.
That's why it would be interesting to see a boat powered by it - to find out more about the unknown generation mechanism at work. It could even be a form of 'static' as electrons drift off and get recollected.

replies.author: @btryba
replies.text: In response to David: You won't ever get anything "perpetual". Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. Kelvin's water dropper uses gravity to separate charges in water. Heron's fountain uses gravity to build pressure to push water up. The closest you'll get is by converting matter into energy, but the government's of the world prefer that common citizens refrain from nuclear endeavors. Robert's use of "perpetual" is correct. The device will exist perpetually minus the natural decay of the materials but that will take a long time.

replies.author: @PressToDigitate
replies.text: @btryba Energy can be extracted from the ambient environment - including, in particular, the Quantum Vacuum - "perpetually", if one creates an Open System.  Mating Kelvin's Water Dropper with the Evaporative Charge Separator which Rob previously demonstrated would represent a system in 'perpetual motion', with gravity and ambient heat (including solar) furnishing unlimited free energy inputs to the system.

text: Looks to me like a magnetohydrodynamic generator which uses the earth’s magnetic field coupled with dissimilar metals to form a galvanic cell.

replies.author: @wanderingzanzey2126

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry and i don't mean to be rude but did you actually watch the video?

replies.author: @GreenCaulerpa
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Yes I did fully watch the video and yes I heard that you stated that there was no apparent corrosion but keep in mind the corrosion at these current densities is miniscule, without electron microscopy probably not even noticable. We are talking about microamps so we‘d probably get electron diffusion into the metal at first which explains the lack of oxidizing agent. And the increasing current when moving or heating is easily explained by magnetohydrodynamic effects directly when moving the metal and indirectly by heat convection caused by a heat gradient. 
Not meaning to be rude but those tests aren’t exactly scientific either and also you can and will get a potential difference when using dissimilar metals, no matter what. That’s electrochemistry 101.

replies.author: @GreenCaulerpa
replies.text: zacariah heim No, all he clearly demonstrated was that it did produce some current. Also I am not saying this wouldn‘t be interesting but its clearly galvanic and magnetohydrodynamic effects that cause the potential difference. He would have the same effects (even though probably less strong) when using two similar metals in a magnetic field.
